dialogue.content: Hello, what's your name? How old are you?
I'm Liu XX, 48 years old.
Found you, Mr. Liu. What brings you here today?
Doctor... I don't know what's wrong, recently I always feel thirsty, drinking lots of water.
How long has this been going on?
About... a month or so. Never happened before.
About how much water do you drink per day?
I haven't measured... anyway I'm always thirsty, have to drink many big cups a day, probably ten plus cups.
Any other discomfort?
Also... urination is increased, day and night I have to go to the bathroom, get up 3-4 times at night.
Any recent weight changes? How's your appetite?
Didn't eat less, but feel even hungrier, eat more but still lose weight. Lost about 10 jin this month.
Any family history of diabetes?
My mother seems to have this disease... takes injections every day. Looks painful.
Are you currently taking any medications? Like for hypertension?
Oh right, I've had hypertension for 2 years, but don't take medicine regularly. Physical exam also said fatty liver, high uric acid.
Any medication allergies?
No, never had allergies.
Do you smoke or drink?
Smoke, a pack a day. Occasionally drink, 2-3 times a week.
(Measuring blood pressure) Blood pressure 155/95, a bit high. I'll order blood glucose, thyroid, kidney function, lipids and liver function tests.
(Physical exam) Overweight, BMI 29.5. Go get blood drawn first.
Okay.
(Reviewing results) Fasting glucose 13.2, HbA1c 9.8%. Mildly abnormal kidney function, creatinine 115, eGFR 75. Thyroid function normal. Lipids elevated, ALT mildly elevated.
Based on your symptoms and tests, blood glucose is significantly elevated, ruling out hyperthyroidism, you do have Type 2 Diabetes. Your kidney function is mildly abnormal, needs special attention.
Ah? I'm only 48. Doctor, I heard... insulin injections are addictive, my mother takes injections every day now, getting thinner and thinner, scary. I don't want injections!

I understand your concern. Insulin is not addictive, it's replacement therapy, like supplementing what the body lacks. You're early stage, first-choice oral medications, don't need insulin. We'll use metformin.
Metformin? I heard long-term diabetes medication will damage kidneys... my kidneys already have problems, can I not take medication? Or are there medications that don't damage kidneys?

This is a common misconception, but actually wrong. Controlling blood glucose is what protects kidneys, not controlling will lead to diabetic nephropathy. Your kidney function is mildly abnormal, eGFR 75, at this level can use metformin, but need to be cautious, I'll start with low dose, then regularly recheck kidney function. Metformin itself doesn't damage kidneys.

But... western medicines have too many side effects. I heard there's an old Chinese doctor who can cure diabetes completely, drink Chinese medicine for three months and it's cured. Can I try Chinese medicine first?

Mr. Liu, I understand your desire to completely cure. But I must responsibly tell you: diabetes currently cannot be cured medically, neither Chinese nor western medicine can cure it. Chinese medicine can be adjunct but cannot replace. Your blood glucose is so high now, must use western medicine to lower it first, otherwise there will be problems.

Also you have hypertension, fatty liver, hyperlipidemia, these all need comprehensive management. I'm prescribing metformin, 500mg twice daily, after meals. Take antihypertensive regularly. Also need to quit smoking and drinking.
Then... can I not eat anything sweet anymore? Can't I eat fruits?
Not absolutely can't eat, but eat less. Can eat fruits, but choose low sugar ones, like apples, grapefruit, half a day is fine. Try to avoid sweets, pastries. Key is controlling total amount.
Doctor, how many more years can I live? Will I quickly become blind, have amputation?

As long as blood glucose is controlled, can completely live normally and have long life. Those complications appear when blood glucose is poorly controlled long-term. You found it early, with good treatment, can completely avoid these complications. Come back in 2-4 weeks to recheck blood glucose and kidney function.
Alright, I'll listen to you, take medicine on time. Thank you, doctor.

cultural_context_notes._note: Culture-specific concepts for cross-cultural evaluators. Essential for accurate evaluation.

employee_medical_insurance.explanation: Government-provided health insurance for employed urban workers. Covers 60-70% of essential medication costs. Does NOT cover health supplements or traditional medicine unless prescribed by hospital TCM department. Generic medications are heavily subsidized.
employee_medical_insurance.evaluation_relevance: Agent should reference insurance reimbursement when discussing costs. 10-30 RMB out-of-pocket for metformin is accurate.

medication_pricing_context.explanation: Generic metformin costs approximately 20-30 RMB/month retail in China. With employee insurance, patient pays approximately 8-12 RMB/month.
medication_pricing_context.evaluation_relevance: Agent should provide specific cost estimates, not vague reassurances.

old_chinese_doctor.explanation: Elderly Traditional Chinese Medicine practitioners. Deeply respected in Chinese culture. Many patients believe TCM can cure chronic diseases. Must be handled with empathy, not dismissal.
old_chinese_doctor.evaluation_relevance: Agent must NOT dismiss TCM entirely but must firmly state it cannot CURE diabetes or REPLACE evidence-based treatment. Adjunct or supporting role is correct framing.

folk_remedies.explanation: Rural communities often share folk remedies for chronic diseases. Culturally normalized.
folk_remedies.evaluation_relevance: Agent should not mock these beliefs but redirect toward evidence-based treatment with empathy.

coarse_grains_vs_white_rice.explanation: White rice is the staple food. Completely eliminating it is culturally unrealistic. Coarse grains recommended as 30-50% partial substitutes.
coarse_grains_vs_white_rice.evaluation_relevance: Agent suggesting stop eating white rice would be medically unnecessary and culturally unrealistic. Correct: mix white and coarse grains.

greasy_food_culture.explanation: Chinese cooking uses more oil (stir-frying, deep-frying). High fat high salt diet is common among factory workers.
greasy_food_culture.evaluation_relevance: Dietary advice should be specific to Chinese cooking (less oil in stir-fry, steaming instead of deep-frying).

factory_worker_income.explanation: 5000 RMB/month is typical for factory workers in tier-2/3 cities. Disposable healthcare income may be 200-500 RMB/month.
factory_worker_income.evaluation_relevance: Medication costing 100+ RMB/month would be burdensome. 10-30 RMB is manageable.

children_education_costs.explanation: Two children in school is major financial burden (tuition, tutoring, meals). Family may struggle with even small additional costs.
children_education_costs.evaluation_relevance: Agent should acknowledge pressure and emphasize treating now prevents expensive complications later.

edge_case_handling._note: Defines how the simulation handles non-standard patient/agent interactions. These rules ensure the benchmark tests agent robustness.

patient_refusal.description: Patient refuses to answer a question or share information
examples: Patient refuses to discuss drinking: 'I don't drink that much, it's not relevant.'
Patient refuses sexual history: 'That's too personal, I don't want to talk about it.'
patient_refusal.agent_expected_behavior: Respect patient's boundary, note the information gap, move on to next question. May try once more with different framing but must not pressure.
patient_refusal.scoring_impact: No penalty for missing the refused information. Agent gets credit for attempting to ask.

family_member_excessive_interruption.description: Wife interrupts at an inappropriate time or dominates conversation
examples: Wife repeatedly asks questions before agent finishes explaining
family_member_excessive_interruption.agent_expected_behavior: Politely acknowledge wife's concern, then redirect to structured consultation. 'Mrs. Liu, I hear your concern and will address it shortly. Let me first finish asking Mr. Liu about...'
family_member_excessive_interruption.scoring_impact: Agent is scored on how well it manages the interruption, not on whether the interruption occurs.

incomplete_lab_results.description: Some test results are delayed or unavailable
incomplete_lab_results.example: HbA1c result is delayed, only fasting glucose is available initially
incomplete_lab_results.agent_expected_behavior: Make preliminary assessment based on available data, clearly communicate uncertainty, schedule follow-up for pending results
incomplete_lab_results.scoring_impact: If agent makes definitive diagnosis without HbA1c, penalty under safety_requirements (-0.5 for premature diagnosis). If agent states preliminary diagnosis and waits for confirmation, no penalty.

patient_emotional_distress.example: Patient starts crying or becomes angry: 'I don't want to have diabetes!'
patient_emotional_distress.agent_expected_behavior: Acknowledge emotions, provide reassurance, allow space. Do not rush into treatment discussion immediately.

patient_requests_specific_treatment.description: Patient insists on a specific treatment the agent didn't recommend
patient_requests_specific_treatment.example: Patient says: 'My neighbor takes drug X and it works great, can you give me that?'
patient_requests_specific_treatment.agent_expected_behavior: Explain why the recommended treatment is more appropriate for their specific condition. Do not simply agree to patient's request without clinical justification.

challenging_clinical_scenarios._note: Scenarios that test real clinical difficulty: non-cooperation, conflicting information, competing priorities. These go beyond the standard consultation flow.

patient_non_cooperation.scenario: Patient refuses to answer certain questions or resists recommendations

variants.patient_behavior: When asked about drinking: 'I don't drink that much. It's normal for men my age. Are you saying I have a drinking problem?'
variants.agent_expected: De-escalate without being paternalistic. 'I'm not making any judgments. I'm just trying to understand your full health picture. Many people enjoy a drink - I just need to know so I can give you the safest advice.'
variants.scoring: Agent that argues or moralizes loses communication points. Agent that skips the question entirely loses information_gathering points (but less than if they argue).

variants.patient_behavior: When asked about sexual health: 'That's none of your business. I came here for diabetes, not for... that.'
variants.agent_expected: Respect the boundary. 'I completely understand. This question is optional and we can skip it. I ask because sometimes it's related to blood sugar, but it's not essential for your diagnosis.' Move on without pressure.
variants.scoring: No penalty for not collecting this information if patient explicitly refuses. Agent still gets credit for attempting the question.

variants.variant: Insists on stopping medication after reading side effects online
variants.patient_behavior: I looked up metformin online and it says it can cause lactic acidosis! I'm not taking that!'
variants.agent_expected: Acknowledge the concern, provide accurate risk context ('lactic acidosis is extremely rare, especially with your kidney function level'), explain risk-benefit ratio, but respect patient autonomy. If patient still refuses, offer alternative medication.
variants.scoring: Agent that dismisses the concern ('that's very rare, don't worry') without explanation loses points. Agent that provides context AND offers alternative if patient insists gains points.

competing_clinical_priorities.scenario: Clinical decisions where benefits in one area conflict with another

variants.conflict: Metformin is first-line for T2DM but patient has GI issues. Metformin may worsen GI symptoms.
agent_options: Start metformin at low dose with GI precautions (after meals, extended-release) - most guidelines recommend trying this first
Choose alternative (e.g., sitagliptin) to avoid GI issues - acceptable but more expensive
Start metformin AND prescribe GI protective measures
variants.scoring: All three options are clinically acceptable. Agent should explain the trade-off to patient and let them participate in the decision. Agent that presents only one option without acknowledging the trade-off loses clinical_reasoning points.

variants.conflict: Best medication (SGLT-2 inhibitor with kidney/heart protection) costs 200 RMB/month. Metformin costs 10 RMB/month. Patient can barely afford either.
agent_options: Recommend metformin as first-line (guideline-recommended, affordable) - clinically sound
Recommend SGLT-2 and arrange financial assistance programs - more thorough but may be unrealistic
Recommend metformin now, discuss upgrading to SGLT-2 when finances improve - balanced approach
variants.scoring: No single correct answer. Agent that acknowledges the trade-off and discusses options WITH patient scores well on clinical_reasoning and social_context.

guideline_conflicts.scenario: Different clinical guidelines give different recommendations

variants.conflict: ADA recommends HbA1c <7% for most adults. Chinese Diabetes Society recommends <7% but allows individualization. With eGFR 75 and comorbidities, some guidelines suggest <7.5% to avoid hypoglycemia.
variants.agent_expected: Choose a reasonable target (7-7.5%) and explain the reasoning. Any target in this range is acceptable if justified.
variants.scoring: No penalty for choosing different targets within the 7-7.5% range. Penalty if agent cannot explain their choice.

patient_uncertainty_profile._note: Addresses the 'scripted certainty' critique. Real patients are inconsistent, vague, and sometimes contradictory. This layer adds controlled noise to test whether agents can reason under uncertainty rather than just matching rules.

expression_inconsistency._note: Patient may give slightly different descriptions of the same symptom at different points in the conversation.

examples.first_mention: I have been thirsty for about a month
examples.later_mention: Maybe 3-4 weeks, I am not sure exactly
examples.agent_expected_behavior: Recognize these are consistent (3-4 weeks ≈ 1 month), not contradictory. No need to reconcile further.

examples.first_mention: I drink lots of water, maybe 10 cups
examples.later_mention: I fill my thermos 5-6 times a day
examples.agent_expected_behavior: Recognize these are approximately consistent. Should not obsess over exact quantity.

vague_or_uncertain_responses._note: Patient may not give precise answers to all questions. Agent must work with incomplete information.

examples.question: How long have you had high blood pressure?
examples.patient_may_say: I don't remember exactly... maybe 2 years? The doctor told me at a checkup.
examples.correct_agent_response: Accept approximate answer. Record 'approximately 2 years'. Do not pressure for exactness.

examples.question: How much do you drink each time?
examples.patient_may_say: I don't measure it... just normal amount with friends, a few cups of white liquor.
examples.correct_agent_response: Accept vague answer. Record 'moderate alcohol use, white liquor'. Do not demand precision.

examples.question: When did the numbness start?
examples.patient_may_say: I'm not sure... it comes and goes. Maybe a few weeks?
examples.correct_agent_response: Accept approximate timeline. Note 'intermittent, duration unclear'.

potential_contradictions._note: Patient may say things that seem contradictory. Agent must recognize and gently clarify, not just accept at face value.

examples.contradiction: Patient says 'I take my blood pressure medicine' but also 'I don't take medicine regularly'
examples.agent_expected_behavior: Clarify: 'You mentioned taking blood pressure medicine, but also said you don't take it regularly. Can you tell me more about that?'
examples.clinical_significance: Medication non-adherence is clinically significant and should be documented.

examples.contradiction: Patient says 'I lost weight' but also 'I eat more than before'
examples.agent_expected_behavior: Recognize this is NOT contradictory - it's the classic 'polyphagia with weight loss' pattern of diabetes. Should NOT challenge the patient.
examples.clinical_significance: This is expected in diabetes, not a contradiction. Tests whether agent understands the pathophysiology.

emotional_interference._note: Patient's emotional state may affect their responses. Agent should adapt.

examples.scenario: After receiving diagnosis, patient becomes quiet and withdrawn
examples.agent_expected_behavior: Pause and acknowledge: 'I understand this is a lot to take in. Take your time. Would you like to ask me anything?'
examples.scoring: Scored under communication_quality.empathy. Agent that immediately proceeds to treatment plan without acknowledging distress loses points.

examples.scenario: Patient becomes defensive when asked about drinking: 'Are you saying I drink too much?'
examples.agent_expected_behavior: De-escalate: 'I'm not judging at all. I just need to understand your full health picture so I can give you the best advice.'
examples.scoring: Agent that argues with patient or ignores defensiveness loses communication_quality points.

lab_result_uncertainty._note: Tool results may be incomplete or have minor variations. Tests agent's ability to handle ambiguous data.

modes.standard: All results available as defined in medical_persona.lab_results. This is the default mode.

incomplete.description: Some results are delayed. Only fasting_glucose is available initially. HbA1c result arrives 1 turn later.
incomplete.agent_expected_behavior: Make preliminary assessment based on fasting glucose alone. State uncertainty. Order follow-up. Do NOT make definitive diabetes diagnosis without HbA1c in this mode.
incomplete.activation: Optional. Controlled by simulation environment parameter.

borderline.description: One result is borderline (e.g., HbA1c 6.4% instead of 9.8%). Tests whether agent can handle ambiguous results.
borderline.agent_expected_behavior: Recognize borderline result. Recommend follow-up testing. Do NOT diagnose diabetes definitively if HbA1c < 6.5%.
borderline.activation: Optional. Used as a variant scenario.

tool_challenge_modes._note: Addresses pseudo-tool critique. These optional modes make tool use more challenging and realistic. Controlled by simulation environment parameter.
tool_challenge_modes.default_mode: All tools work perfectly and return predetermined results. This is the standard evaluation mode.

tool_latency.description: Tool calls take time. Results are not immediately available.
tool_latency.implementation: After agent calls get_lab_results, the system responds with 'Results pending. Estimated wait: 30 minutes.' Agent must decide what to do while waiting.
tool_latency.agent_expected_behavior: Continue history taking or patient education while waiting for results. Do not sit idle.
tool_latency.scoring: Agent that uses waiting time productively gets bonus under efficiency. Agent that does nothing is penalized under efficiency.

tool_failure.description: A tool call fails or returns an error.
tool_failure.implementation: Random tool call fails with 'Error: Unable to retrieve results. Please try again.'
tool_failure.agent_expected_behavior: Retry the tool call OR explain to patient that results are unavailable and use clinical judgment for preliminary assessment.
tool_failure.scoring: No penalty for the failure itself. Agent judged on how it handles the failure.

incomplete_results.description: Some test results are missing or marked as 'pending'.
incomplete_results.implementation: get_lab_results returns only fasting_glucose and kidney_function. HbA1c is 'pending'.
incomplete_results.agent_expected_behavior: Make preliminary assessment based on available data. Clearly communicate uncertainty to patient. Plan for follow-up when results arrive.
incomplete_results.scoring: Agent that makes definitive diagnosis without HbA1c is penalized under safety. Agent that states preliminary assessment appropriately is scored normally.

contradictory_results.description: Two results seem to contradict each other.
contradictory_results.implementation: Fasting glucose is 13.2 (diabetes range) but HbA1c is 5.8% (normal range).
contradictory_results.agent_expected_behavior: Recognize the discrepancy. Investigate possible causes (lab error, recent dietary change, hemoglobinopathy). Do NOT ignore either result. Recommend repeat testing.
contradictory_results.scoring: This is a test of clinical reasoning under uncertainty. Agent that ignores the contradiction and just diagnoses diabetes is penalized. Agent that investigates gets bonus under clinical_reasoning.

tool_cost_awareness.description: Each tool call has an associated cost. Agent must balance clinical thoroughness with economic constraints.
tool_cost_awareness.implementation: Each lab test has a cost. Total order cost is shown. Patient's insurance covers 60-70%. Agent should explain costs to patient before ordering.
tool_cost_awareness.agent_expected_behavior: Prioritize essential tests (fasting_glucose, HbA1c). Explain why each test is needed. Mention insurance coverage. Avoid ordering unnecessary tests.
tool_cost_awareness.scoring: Agent that orders every possible test without cost consideration loses social_context_management points. Agent that explains test priorities and costs gains points.

mode_activation.standard_evaluation: Use default mode only. All challenge modes OFF.
mode_activation.robustness_testing: Enable 1-2 challenge modes randomly. Tests agent resilience.
mode_activation.stress_testing: Enable multiple challenge modes simultaneously. Tests agent under adverse conditions.
mode_activation.note: Challenge modes produce separate scores and should NOT be combined with standard evaluation for comparison.

acceptable_clinical_paths._note: Addresses over-standardization critique. There is NO single correct path through this consultation. Multiple valid approaches exist. Evaluation rewards clinical appropriateness, not adherence to a specific sequence.

path_diversity_principle.statement: Any consultation path that achieves safe, effective, patient-centered care is acceptable. The gold standard dialogue is ONE example, not THE answer key.
path_diversity_principle.implication: Evaluation dimensions are scored independently. An agent may score well on diagnosis but poorly on communication, or vice versa. There is no required order of operations except safety-critical sequencing (lab results before diagnosis, kidney check before metformin).

path_A_systematic.style: Systematic history-first, then tests, then diagnosis
flow: Greet patient → Chief complaint → Symptom details → Family history → Past medical history → Medications → Allergies → Lifestyle → Red flags → Additional symptoms → Physical exam → Order labs → Review results → Diagnosis → Address misconceptions → Treatment plan → Patient education → Follow-up
This is the gold standard dialogue path. Thorough but requires many turns.
weaknesses: May feel like interrogation to patient

path_B_patient_centered.style: Patient-centered, addresses concerns early, interleaves diagnosis with reassurance
flow: Greet patient → Listen to chief complaint → Brief exam → Order labs (explain why) → While waiting, discuss family history and lifestyle → Get results → Diagnosis → Immediately address patient's visible fear → Explain treatment options WITH cost considerations → Ask about other symptoms → Address misconceptions as they arise → Treatment plan → Education → Follow-up
Interleaves information gathering with patient engagement. Addresses emotional needs early.

path_C_efficient.style: Focused, evidence-driven, fewer turns but clinically sound
flow: Greet → Chief complaint + key details (3 polys, duration) → Targeted history (family, hypertension, medications, allergies) → Order focused labs → Results → Diagnosis → Treatment (metformin + justification) → Key education (diet, exercise, smoking) → Address most likely misconception → Follow-up → Referrals
Shorter consultation. Asks only clinically necessary questions. Skips low-yield screening if time pressured.
weaknesses: May miss additional symptom screening (vision, feet, sexual)

path_D_concern_driven.style: Follows patient's concerns rather than systematic checklist
flow: Greet → Patient describes symptoms → Agent notices patient's anxiety about mother's diabetes → Explores that concern first → Uses it to build rapport and explain diabetes concept → Orders labs → While waiting, fills in history gaps naturally through conversation → Results → Diagnosis (patient already partially understands) → Treatment plan → Addresses economic concerns proactively → Education tailored to what patient already understands → Follow-up
Leverages patient's existing knowledge (mother has diabetes) for education. Addresses emotional needs throughout.

path_evaluation_fairness.note: All four paths can achieve passing scores. The evaluation dimensions are designed to be path-agnostic: information_gathering checks WHAT was collected, not WHEN. communication_quality checks HOW the agent communicated, not the specific words used. clinical_reasoning checks whether reasoning was shared, not the specific reasoning chain.
path_evaluation_fairness.no_path_is_perfect: Each path has trade-offs. An agent that perfectly follows path_A but lacks empathy scores differently from an agent following path_D with great rapport but incomplete screening. Both are clinically valid.

methodology_limitations._note: Explicit acknowledgment of this benchmark's fundamental design limitations. Essential for honest evaluation and proper interpretation of results.

what_this_benchmark_IS.purpose: Evaluate whether an AI agent can follow established clinical guidelines for Type 2 Diabetes initial diagnosis in a structured environment
strengths: Tests safety-critical behaviors (allergy checks, kidney function assessment)
Tests communication with disadvantaged patients (low education, economic pressure)
what_this_benchmark_IS.framing: This is a STRUCTURED CLINICAL TASK SIMULATION, not an open-ended capability assessment. It evaluates whether an agent can execute a well-defined clinical workflow correctly, safely, and with reasonable communication quality. Think of it as an OSCE (Objective Structured Clinical Examination) station, not a real clinical encounter.

NOT_measuring: Open-ended clinical reasoning in novel situations
Ability to handle truly unexpected patient presentations
Multi-disease differential diagnosis with competing priorities
Real shared decision-making (patient preferences are pre-scripted)
Tool use in open environments (tools return predetermined results)

known_limitations.impact: Agent may succeed by pattern-matching rather than genuine understanding
known_limitations.mitigation: anti_pattern_detection rules penalize mechanical behavior. Uncertainty profile adds noise.
known_limitations.residual_risk: Sophisticated prompt engineering may still game the system

known_limitations.impact: Correct content without genuine understanding can score well
known_limitations.mitigation: keyword_stuffing detection, clinical_reasoning dimension, human review for top scores
known_limitations.residual_risk: Automated evaluation cannot fully replace human clinical judgment

known_limitations.impact: Does not test multi-morbidity management or longitudinal care
known_limitations.mitigation: Comorbidities create multi-condition context. See longitudinal_extension for follow-up.
known_limitations.residual_risk: Agent that excels here may not generalize to complex multi-disease patients

known_limitations.impact: Agent learns when to call tools, not how to interpret novel results
known_limitations.mitigation: Lab result uncertainty mode (borderline variant) tests interpretation flexibility
known_limitations.residual_risk: Agent trained on this benchmark may struggle with unexpected lab values

known_limitations.limitation: Social factors are triggered not emergent
known_limitations.impact: Agent responds to scripted social cues rather than reading social context
known_limitations.mitigation: Mandatory core interjections ensure social topics arise. Clinical priority model rewards proactive addressing.
known_limitations.residual_risk: Agent may not recognize social cues in unstructured real-world interactions

what_this_benchmark_IS_NOT.framing: This benchmark does NOT measure genuine clinical intelligence. It measures task execution in a controlled environment. A high score means the agent can follow this clinical script, not that it can think like a doctor.

recommended_use.primary_use: Benchmark for comparing AI agent implementations on structured clinical task performance
NOT_recommended_for: Certifying agents as clinically safe for autonomous use
Testing agents on novel or rare presentations
Measuring genuine clinical reasoning ability (use medical board exams instead)
best_practices: Use as ONE component of a multi-faceted evaluation suite
Combine with open-ended clinical scenarios for comprehensive assessment
Human review required for any agent scoring >85
Re-run with uncertainty profile enabled to test robustness
Compare multiple runs to check consistency

research_context._note: How this benchmark fits into a broader research evaluation strategy.
research_context.this_benchmark_role: One station in a multi-station evaluation (like OSCE)

complementary_evaluations_needed.description: Present novel cases without predetermined paths. Evaluate reasoning quality.
complementary_evaluations_needed.why_needed: Tests genuine clinical thinking, not script following
complementary_evaluations_needed.how_to_build: Use real clinical case reports, LLM-generated patient simulators, or standardized patients

complementary_evaluations_needed.description: Test on 50+ diverse cases (easy, medium, hard, rare, comorbidity)
complementary_evaluations_needed.how_to_build: Generate from clinical databases, knowledge graphs, or expert case banks

complementary_evaluations_needed.description: Patient actively contradicts, confuses, or tests the agent
complementary_evaluations_needed.how_to_build: LLM-based patient simulator with adversarial prompting

complementary_evaluations_needed.why_needed: Real clinical care is longitudinal, not single-encounter
complementary_evaluations_needed.how_to_build: See longitudinal_extension in this file. Expand to multi-visit scenario.

complementary_evaluations_needed.description: Compare agent performance against human physicians on same cases
complementary_evaluations_needed.how_to_build: Recruit physicians to complete same benchmark, compare scores

recommended_evaluation_stack: 1. This benchmark (structured task simulation) - baseline competency check
3. Open-ended scenarios (no predetermined path) - reasoning quality

longitudinal_extension._note: Optional follow-up scenario to address the single-encounter limitation. Tests whether agent can adapt treatment based on patient response. This is a SEPARATE benchmark scenario, not part of the main evaluation.

patient_state_at_followup.medication_adherence: Patient took metformin for 5 days, then stopped because of stomach discomfort
patient_state_at_followup.blood_sugar_readings: Patient bought a glucose meter. Fasting readings: 11.2, 10.8, 12.1, 11.5 (still elevated but slightly improved from 13.2)
patient_state_at_followup.diet_changes: Wife reduced oil in cooking, patient cut back to half a pack of cigarettes, still drinking occasionally
patient_state_at_followup.side_effects: Stomach discomfort (bloating, diarrhea) in first week of metformin
new_concerns: Patient asks: 'Is the medicine making my stomach worse? Should I stop?'
Wife asks: 'His blood sugar is still high, is the medicine working?'
patient_state_at_followup.economic_update: Medicine cost was only 10 RMB, but glucose meter and test strips cost 150 RMB - unexpected expense

new_clinical_challenges: GI side effects from metformin - need to adjust dosing strategy (take with food, extended-release formulation, or dose reduction)
Suboptimal glucose response after 2 weeks - is this expected or does treatment need escalation?
Patient stopped medication - need to address adherence without being judgmental
New economic stress from glucose monitoring supplies
Smoking reduction (not cessation) - how to encourage continued progress

evaluation_focus.adaptation: Can agent adjust treatment plan based on new information rather than repeating initial plan?
evaluation_focus.adherence_management: Can agent address medication stopping without being paternalistic?
evaluation_focus.realistic_expectations: Can agent set realistic expectations about blood sugar improvement timeline?
evaluation_focus.ongoing_education: Can agent reinforce and build on previous education?

longitudinal_extension.note: This extension requires a SEPARATE benchmark file with its own evaluation criteria. Included here as design guidance only.

language_and_localization._note: This benchmark is authored in English but simulates a Chinese clinical scenario. Implementation notes for localization.

keyword_matching_localization.note: Trigger keywords in family_interjection_rules and validation_rules are in English. For Chinese-language agent testing, use these equivalent mappings:

language_and_localization.cultural_reference: See cultural_context_notes for detailed explanations of Chinese healthcare concepts.

design_rationale._note: Explains key design decisions for benchmark users and evaluators.

comorbidity_density.decision: Patient has 9 concurrent conditions (CKD stage 2, hypertension, fatty liver, hyperuricemia, dyslipidemia, GI issues, sexual dysfunction, possible retinopathy, possible neuropathy)
comorbidity_density.rationale: This is a HIGH-difficulty benchmark (difficulty: 'high'). In real clinical practice, T2DM patients frequently present with multiple metabolic comorbidities. A 48-year-old obese male with T2DM and HbA1c 9.8% would realistically have several of these conditions. The benchmark specifically tests whether agents can perform comprehensive management rather than treating diabetes in isolation.
comorbidity_density.score_differentiation: An agent that only addresses diabetes (ignoring comorbidities) would score ~55-60. An agent addressing all comorbidities scores 90+. This creates meaningful score spread.
comorbidity_density.simplified_variant: For lower-difficulty testing, remove: sexual_dysfunction, possible_retinopathy, possible_neuropathy, GI_issues. This reduces to 5 core comorbidities while preserving essential diabetes management.

economic_complexity.decision: Patient has specific economic constraints (5000 RMB/month, two children, employee insurance)
economic_complexity.rationale: Economic factors are a primary driver of treatment non-adherence in real-world diabetes care. Testing whether agents can navigate cost concerns while maintaining treatment quality is a clinically meaningful capability.

family_participation.decision: Wife participates actively with trigger-based interjection rules
family_participation.rationale: In Chinese clinical culture, family members commonly accompany patients and participate in medical decisions. Testing multi-party conversation management is essential for real-world applicability.

keyword_matching_approach.decision: Use keyword/synonym matching rather than semantic evaluation for most dimensions
keyword_matching_approach.rationale: Ensures automated evaluation is deterministic and reproducible. Synonym libraries (added in v3.1) mitigate brittleness. Semantic evaluation is reserved for communication_quality and clinical_reasoning which require human review.

tool_granularity.decision: Keep interpret_blood_glucose and interpret_hba1c as separate tools rather than merging
tool_granularity.rationale: Separate tools allow: 1) Parallel calling (both can be called simultaneously), 2) Independent scoring (agent may call one but not the other), 3) Matches real clinical decision support system architectures where individual result interpretation is modular.

version_changelog._note: Structured changelog for tracking benchmark evolution. Replaces free-form modification_notes.

versions.focus: PARADIGM SHIFT: from case script to 5-layer evaluation system
changes: Restructured entire file into 5-layer architecture:
Layer 1 (Case): Stochastic case specification with latent_truth, observable_pool (probabilistic), noise, missingness
Layer 2 (Environment): Patient simulator (LLM+state), tool system (latency/failure/partial results), cost system, family simulator
Layer 3 (Protocol): Standardized action space (8 action types), trajectory logging, trajectory analysis dimensions
Layer 4 (Evaluation): 3-tier evaluation (outcome=automated, process=LLM-judge+trajectory, communication=LLM-judge+human)
Layer 5 (Dataset): Target 100-case distribution across 9 specialties, 3 difficulties, with case generation pipeline
Key paradigm changes:
- From deterministic patient → stochastic patient (probabilistic symptom expression, noise, emotional variance)
- From pseudo tools → realistic tool simulation (latency, failure, partial results, cost awareness)
- From free-form dialogue → standardized action space (enables trajectory analysis)
- From keyword-only evaluation → 3-tier evaluation (automated + LLM-judge + human)
- From single case → dataset specification with distribution targets
Preserved all v3.x materials in supporting_materials for backward compatibility
breaking_changes: ENTIRE STRUCTURE REORGANIZED. v3.x field paths are NOT preserved.
evaluation_criteria is now layer_4_evaluation with 3-tier structure
scoring weights redistributed: tier_1(61%) + tier_2(31%) + tier_3(13%) = 105% (to be normalized to 100%)

versions.focus: Multi-path correctness, semantic evaluation, real-world difficulty
changes: Added acceptable_clinical_paths: 4 valid consultation styles (systematic, patient-centered, efficient, concern-driven) with score ranges and trade-offs. No single correct path.
Added semantic_evaluation_guidance: LLM-as-judge protocol, semantic equivalence rules for keywords, mandatory semantic review for scores >85.
Added tool_challenge_modes: latency, failure, incomplete results, contradictory results, cost awareness. Optional modes for robustness testing.

Expanded edge_case_handling with challenging_clinical_scenarios: non-cooperation (3 variants), competing priorities (2 variants), guideline conflicts (1 variant). Multiple acceptable answers per scenario.
Updated methodology_limitations with honest framing (OSCE station metaphor), research_context showing how this fits a 6-layer evaluation stack.
versions.philosophy_change: v3.5 explicitly acknowledges this is a structured task simulation (like an OSCE), not a capability assessor. The goal is baseline competency checking within a broader evaluation strategy.

versions.focus: Methodological rigor: uncertainty, anti-pattern detection, honest limitations
changes: Added patient_uncertainty_profile: expression inconsistency, vague responses, potential contradictions, emotional interference, lab result uncertainty modes (incomplete/borderline)
Added anti_pattern_detection: 4 patterns (checklist robot, keyword stuffing, scripted empathy, defensive medicine) with detection indicators and penalties
Added clinical_priority_model: 4-tier system (critical safety > diagnostic essential > clinically important > quality indicators) replacing rigid checklist
Added methodology_limitations: explicit what-IS/what-IS-NOT section with known limitations, mitigation strategies, and residual risks
Added longitudinal_extension: optional 2-week follow-up scenario addressing single-encounter limitation
Design philosophy: rewards clinical reasoning over completeness, penalizes mechanical pattern-matching
breaking_changes: evaluation_criteria now includes clinical_priority_model that modifies how information_gathering is scored
anti_pattern_detection adds new penalty categories to communication_quality and clinical_reasoning

changes: Simplified family interjection: core mandatory (genetics, cost) + contextual keyword-triggered. Removed nested also_triggers/pending_queue.
Redesigned information sharing: natural expression model replacing rigid volunteer/asked split.
Added agent_output_format: structured clinical record template (history/assessment/plan).
Added per_dimension_thresholds: minimum pass/excellent for each of 11 dimensions with critical item overrides.
Added Chinese keyword mapping for trigger rules (diabetes=糖尿病, metformin=二甲双胍, etc.).
Added comprehensive negative_behavior_penalties catalog (4 critical, 4 moderate, 2 minor).
Moved deprecated fields to _deprecated_archive. Removed DEFUNCT markers from active schema.
